title: Atrahasis (Akkadian flood epic)
translation_source: Composite public-domain renderings after King, Thompson, and Budge (creation & flood tablets)

paragraphs.translation: The gods had to dig out canals, they had to clear channels, the lifelines of the land. The gods dug the Tigris river and the Euphrates river, and they dug irrigation ditches. For forty years they endured this labor; the vast earth resounded with their groans.

Enlil, counselor of the gods, Ellil, set the other gods to digging. The Igigi gods dug the riverbeds, but the work was too hard. They burned their tools and surrounded the house of Enlil in the night, saying: Let us confront our father Enlil, let us get relief from our labor.

Ea made ready to speak and said to the gods his brothers: Why are we making war? Their labor is indeed heavy; their distress is too much! Let the birth goddess create a mortal man so that he may bear the yoke of the gods. Let man assume the drudgery of the god.

They slaughtered Aw-ilu, who had the inspiration, in their assembly; Nintu mixed clay with his flesh and blood. They pinched off fourteen pieces of clay and set seven on the right, seven on the left. The birth goddess opened the womb and mankind was created.

Nintu made them bear the load of the gods. But the noise of mankind disturbed Enlil. He could not sleep because of the clamor. Enlil could not rest by day or sleep by night. The gods complained to Enlil: The people have multiplied; their noise is too loud.
Enlil ordered a plague to reduce the people. Atra-hasis was told in a dream how to appease the god with offerings. When the plague ended, the people multiplied again and the noise returned.
Enlil then withheld the rains; famine came upon the land. Again Atra-hasis was warned in a dream. The people survived, but once more they multiplied until the earth was loud again.

Enlil addressed the assembly of the gods: The noise of mankind is intolerable; sleep is no longer possible because of them. So the gods agreed to exterminate mankind. Enlil did not let Ea speak the plan openly; Ea warned Atra-hasis indirectly through a reed wall.

Ea opened his mouth and spoke to his servant: Tear down your house, build a boat; abandon possessions and seek life. Board the boat and save living things. The boat which you build shall have equal dimensions in her length and breadth. Roof it over like the Apsu.

Atra-hasis brought into the boat his silver and gold, living creatures of every kind, the beasts of the field, the creatures of the steppe, and his family. The storm came; for seven days and seven nights the flood swept the land. Even the gods were terrified at the flood; they cowered like dogs.

When the flood had ended and the people had not been seen, the gods gathered like flies over the sacrificer. Enlil was furious that any humans had survived. Ea spoke and rebuked Enlil for sending the flood without counsel. Enlil then blessed Atra-hasis and his wife, making them like gods, granting them eternal life in the distance.
